extra.message:
Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled (Global Configuration)

Jinja2 is configured with `autoescape=False`, which disables HTML escaping globally for all templates. Any user-controlled data rendered in templates will be output as raw HTML, enabling stored and reflected XSS attacks across the entire application.

Impact: Attackers can inject arbitrary JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or further exploitation.

Fix: Set `autoescape=True` (or use `autoescape=select_autoescape(['html', 'jinja2'])`) in the Jinja2 setup call.

SQL Injection via String Interpolation in Student.create()

The `Student.create()` method constructs the SQL INSERT query using Python's `%` string interpolation operator directly on the query string before passing it to `cur.execute()`. The `name` parameter is interpolated into the SQL string at the Python level (not by the database driver), bypassing parameterization entirely. An attacker supplying a crafted `name` value (e.g., `'); DROP TABLE students; --`) can execute arbitrary SQL.

Impact: An attacker can execute arbitrary SQL statements, leading to data exfiltration, data destruction, authentication bypass, or full database compromise.

Fix: Use parameterized queries with the database driver's placeholder mechanism. Replace with: `q = "INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)"` and `await cur.execute(q, (name,))`.

XSS via Unescaped auth_user name fields in base.jinja2

The authenticated user's name fields (`auth_user.first_name`, `auth_user.middle_name`, `auth_user.last_name`) are rendered without escaping in the navigation bar. These are database-stored values that could contain injected HTML/JavaScript.

Impact: Stored XSS: If a user's name fields contain malicious JavaScript, it executes on every page load for that user (and potentially others if admin can view user names).

Fix: Use `{{ auth_user.first_name | e }}` etc., or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped review_text in course.jinja2

The `review.review_text` field is rendered directly without escaping: `{{ review.review_text }}`. Since autoescape is globally disabled and this field is user-supplied (submitted via the review form), any HTML/JavaScript injected by a user will be rendered as raw HTML to all viewers of the course page.

Impact: Stored XSS: Any user can submit a review containing malicious JavaScript that executes in the browser of every user who views the course page, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or further attacks.

Fix: Use `{{ review.review_text | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in courses.jinja2

The `course.title` field is rendered without escaping in the courses listing: `{{ course.title }}`. With autoescape disabled, this is a stored XSS vector.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, executing in the browser of any user viewing the courses list.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped student.name in evaluate.jinja2 (aside block)

The `student.name` field is rendered without escaping in the aside block: `Back to student {{ student.name }}`. While the breadcrumb uses `| e`, this instance does not.

Impact: Stored XSS via student name, executing in the browser of any user viewing the evaluate error page.

Fix: Use `{{ student.name | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in review.jinja2

The `course.title` field is rendered without escaping in the review form: `Write review to {{ course.title }}`. With autoescape disabled, this is a stored XSS vector.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, executing in the browser of any user visiting the review submission page.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped course.title in student.jinja2

The `course.title` field is rendered without escaping in the student detail page: `{{ course.title }}`. With autoescape disabled, this is a stored XSS vector.

Impact: Stored XSS via course title, executing in the browser of any user viewing a student's results page.

Fix: Use `{{ course.title | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

XSS via Unescaped student name in students.jinja2

Student names are rendered without escaping in the students list: `{{ name }}`. With autoescape disabled, a student name containing HTML/JavaScript will execute in the browser.

Impact: Stored XSS via student name, executing in the browser of any user viewing the students list.

Fix: Use `{{ name | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

Missing Authentication on POST /courses/ (Create Course)

The `courses` handler accepts POST requests to create new courses without any authentication check. There is no call to `get_auth_user`, `@authorize()`, or any session check in the POST branch. The template shows the form only to admin users (`{% if auth_user and auth_user.is_admin %}`), but this is a frontend-only control that does not protect the endpoint.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can create arbitrary course records in the database by sending a direct POST request to `/courses/`.

Fix: Add `@authorize(ensure_admin=True)` decorator to the `courses` handler, or add an explicit authentication and admin check in the POST branch.

Cleartext Storage of Database Password in Configuration File

The database password is stored in cleartext in the YAML configuration file.

Impact: Plaintext credentials in configuration files can be read by anyone with file system access, in logs, or via accidental exposure (e.g., version control).

Fix: Use environment variable substitution or a secrets manager. Never store passwords in plaintext configuration files.

Redis Connection Without Authentication (Config File)

The Redis configuration in dev.yaml has no password field, confirming the Redis instance is accessed without authentication.

Impact: Unauthenticated access to Redis allows reading/modifying session tokens, enabling session hijacking.

Fix: Configure Redis with a strong password and add the password to the configuration.

CSRF Middleware Commented Out

The CSRF middleware (`csrf_middleware`) is commented out in the application middleware stack. All state-changing POST endpoints (login, logout, create student, create course, evaluate, review) are unprotected against Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks. Although CSRF tokens are generated and embedded in forms, they are never validated because the middleware that performs validation is disabled.

Impact: An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits forged POST requests on behalf of authenticated users, performing actions such as creating students/courses, submitting evaluations, or logging users out.

Fix: Uncomment `csrf_middleware` in the middleware list in sqli/app.py to re-enable CSRF protection.

Insecure Password Hashing with MD5

The `User.check_password()` method hashes passwords using MD5 (`hashlib.md5`). MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash function that is extremely fast to compute, making it trivial to crack via brute force or rainbow table attacks. There is no salt applied, making all identical passwords produce the same hash.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords can be rapidly cracked using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force, exposing user credentials and enabling account takeover.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a modern, slow password hashing algorithm such as `bcrypt`, `argon2`, or `scrypt` (available via `passlib` or `argon2-cffi`). Always use a unique per-user salt.

Session Cookie httponly=False

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, meaning the session cookie is accessible to JavaScript running in the browser. This makes session cookies vulnerable to theft via XSS attacks.

Impact: If an XSS vulnerability is exploited (which is highly likely given autoescape=False), an attacker can steal the session cookie via `document.cookie` and hijack authenticated sessions.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` in the RedisStorage configuration to prevent JavaScript access to the session cookie.

Redis Connection Without Authentication (No Password)

The Redis connection is established using only `host`, `port`, and `db` configuration values. No `password` parameter is passed to `aioredis.create_pool()`. If the Redis instance requires authentication (which it should in production), the connection will fail; if Redis is configured without a password (common in development/misconfigured deployments), the session store is accessible to anyone with network access to the Redis port.

Impact: An attacker with network access to the Redis instance can read, modify, or delete session data, enabling session hijacking, privilege escalation, or denial of service.

Fix: Add a `password` field to the Redis configuration and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()`: `redis = await aioredis.create_pool((conf['host'], conf['port']), db=conf['db'], password=conf.get('password'))`.

XSS via Unescaped error.__dict__ in 50x.jinja2

The server error template renders `{{ error.__dict__ }}` without escaping. If any exception attribute contains user-controlled data (e.g., from a URL or request parameter that caused the error), this could be exploited for reflected XSS. Additionally, this exposes internal error details to users.

Impact: Information disclosure of internal server error details, and potential reflected XSS if exception attributes contain user-controlled data.

Fix: Use `{{ error.__dict__ | e }}` and avoid exposing internal error details to end users. Show a generic error message instead.

Missing Rate Limiting on POST / (Login Endpoint)

The login handler (POST to `/`) has no rate limiting or brute-force protection. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force credentials.

Impact: Attackers can brute-force user credentials without any throttling or lockout mechanism.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint (e.g., per-IP or per-username lockout after N failed attempts).

Session Not Regenerated After Login (Session Fixation)

After successful authentication, the session ID is not regenerated. The existing session (which may have been established before login) is reused with the new `user_id` set. This allows session fixation attacks where an attacker pre-establishes a session ID and waits for a victim to authenticate with it.

Impact: An attacker who can set a victim's session cookie (e.g., via network interception or XSS) can fix the session ID before login and then use that same session ID to access the victim's authenticated session after they log in.

Fix: Invalidate and regenerate the session after successful authentication. With aiohttp-session, create a new session or clear and reinitialize the existing one.

Missing Authentication on Students POST Endpoint (Create Student)

The `students` view handler allows any unauthenticated user to create a new student via POST request. There is no authentication or authorization check before processing the POST data and calling `Student.create()`.

Impact: Any unauthenticated user can create arbitrary student records in the database, polluting data and potentially enabling further attacks.

Fix: Add authentication and authorization checks (e.g., require admin role) before processing POST requests to create students. Use the `@authorize()` decorator or check `get_auth_user(request)` at the start of the handler.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production Application

The aiohttp Application is initialized with `debug=True`. This enables verbose error output, detailed tracebacks, and potentially additional debug endpoints that expose internal application details.

Impact: Detailed error messages and stack traces may be exposed to end users, revealing internal application structure, file paths, and configuration details that aid attackers.

Fix: Set `debug=False` in production, or make it configurable via the application config (e.g., read from config/environment variable).

pwd_hash Field Exposed in User NamedTuple

The `User` NamedTuple includes the `pwd_hash` field, which is fetched from the database and stored in the user object. If this object is serialized, logged, or passed to templates, the password hash (even if weak MD5) is unnecessarily exposed. The hash should not be part of the general-purpose user object returned to application layers.

Impact: Password hashes may be inadvertently exposed in logs, error messages, API responses, or template rendering, facilitating offline password cracking attacks.

Fix: Separate authentication concerns from the user data model. Use a dedicated authentication method that fetches and compares the hash without storing it in the general-purpose User object, or exclude `pwd_hash` from the NamedTuple used outside the authentication context.

Redis Configuration Missing Password Authentication

The Redis configuration schema does not include a `password` field. Redis is used for session storage, and without authentication, any process with network access to the Redis instance can read, modify, or delete session data.

Impact: An attacker with network access to Redis can read all session tokens, forge sessions, or delete sessions to cause denial of service.

Fix: Add a `password` field to the Redis config schema and configure Redis with `requirepass` authentication.

Database Connection String Without TLS/SSL

The PostgreSQL DSN is constructed without any SSL/TLS parameters (e.g., `sslmode=require`). Database traffic including credentials, queries, and result sets (which may contain PII) is transmitted in cleartext over the network.

Impact: Network eavesdroppers can intercept database credentials and sensitive PII data transmitted between the application and the database server.

Fix: Add `sslmode=require` (or `sslmode=verify-full` for maximum security) to the DSN: `'dbname={database} user={user} password={password} host={host} port={port} sslmode=require'`.

XSS via Unescaped last_visited in index.jinja2

The `last_visited` value is rendered without escaping: `{{ last_visited }}`. This value is stored in the session and set from `datetime.now().isoformat()`, which is not user-controlled. However, if the session storage were tampered with or the value source changed, this would be an XSS vector. With autoescape=False, any session-stored string would render as raw HTML.

Impact: Low immediate risk since the value comes from datetime, but represents a defense-in-depth failure. If session data is tampered with, XSS is possible.

Fix: Use `{{ last_visited | e }}` or enable global autoescape.

Missing Rate Limiting on POST /students/{student_id}/evaluate/{course_id}

The evaluate endpoint has no rate limiting. An attacker can flood the endpoint with mark assignments.

Impact: Database flooding or manipulation of student grades through mass mark creation.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on state-changing endpoints.

auth_user Full Model Object Passed to Template Context in index view

The `index` view passes the full `auth_user` User model object to the template context, which may contain sensitive fields like password hash.

Impact: The full User object including password hash is available in the template context and could be accidentally rendered or accessed.

Fix: Pass only necessary user fields to the template context rather than the full model object.

Unsafe LIMIT/OFFSET Query Construction in Course.get_many() with Extra '+' Operator

The `Course.get_many()` method has the same spurious `+` operator pattern as `Student.get_many()`. Additionally, `cur.execute(q, **params)` unpacks the params dict as keyword arguments rather than passing it as a positional argument, which is incorrect usage of the aiopg cursor API and may cause runtime errors or silently ignore parameters.

Impact: The `**params` unpacking may cause the parameters to not be properly bound, potentially resulting in the raw `%(limit)s` placeholder being sent to the database, causing an error or unexpected behavior. If parameters are silently ignored, the query may run without LIMIT/OFFSET constraints.

Fix: Pass params as a positional argument: `await cur.execute(q, params)`. Remove the spurious `+` operator from the LIMIT and OFFSET clauses.
